---
title: "Sphere–Polymer Micelle"
subtitle: "Distributed surface references and shared parameters"
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  error: false
---

This model attaches six Gaussian-polymer chains to distributed points on a
solid-sphere surface. Complete the [diblock tutorial](diblock-copolymer.qmd)
first to install Python and learn the basic `World` workflow.

## References and tags

Every `surface#anchorN` label creates a distinct distributed attachment point.
The tag `chain` makes all six arms share `beta_chain` and `Rg_chain`.

## Python

### 1. Create the world and spherical core

Import the required packages, then start a graph with one solid sphere named
`core`. Its surface will provide the attachment points for the chains.

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pyseb

world = pyseb.World("MicelleTutorial")
graph = world.Add("SolidSphere", "core")
```

### 2. Attach six polymer chains

Each loop iteration creates a Gaussian polymer. `surface#anchorN` requests a
distinct distributed point on the core surface. The final argument, `chain`,
is a tag that makes all six polymers share one parameter set.

```{python}
for index in range(6):
    world.Link(
        "GaussianPolymer",
        f"chain{index}.end1",
        f"core.surface#anchor{index}",
        "chain",
    )
```

The subunits still have unique names such as `chain0` and `chain1`, but their
physical symbols use the shared suffix `chain`.

### 3. Name and parameterize the micelle

Name the complete graph before evaluating it. The core has a radius `R_core`,
whereas each Gaussian chain is described by `Rg_chain`.

```{python}
world.Add(graph, "micelle")
parameters = {
    "beta_core": 1.0,
    "R_core": 30.0,
    "beta_chain": 0.25,
    "Rg_chain": 12.0,
}
```

### 4. Evaluate the form factor

Evaluate the named structure over a logarithmic q grid. The calculation
includes the sphere, all six chains, and every interference term implied by
their connections.

```{python}
q = np.geomspace(1e-3, 0.5, 150)
form_factor = np.asarray(
    world.EvaluateFormFactor("micelle", parameters, q.tolist())
)

print("subunits: 1 core + 6 chains")
print(f"F({q[0]:g}) = {form_factor[0]:.8f}")
```

The small-q value is close to one, confirming the expected normalization of
the complete structure.

### 5. Plot the result

```{python}
#| label: fig-python-micelle
#| fig-cap: "Normalized form factor of the sphere–polymer micelle."

plt.loglog(q, form_factor)
plt.xlabel(r"$q$")
plt.ylabel(r"$F(q)$")
plt.show()
```

The curve changes as q resolves first the overall micelle and then the core
and polymer length scales.

## C++

```cpp
{{< include cpp/micelle.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
from _helpers import run_tutorial

run_tutorial("micelle")
```

The output has the same small-q normalization as the Python result. The exact
sampled q values differ only to keep the terminal table compact.

## Next steps

- [Build reusable nested structures](nested-structures.qmd)
- [Naming and linking reference](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Naming-and-Linking)
- [Solid sphere subunit](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Subunit-Solid-Sphere)
